intentional_test_features: Classic acute pericarditis: sharp pleuritic retrosternal pain worse lying flat and on inspiration, relieved by sitting forward, radiating to the trapezius ridge, after a viral prodrome, with a pericardial friction rub

Must-exclude mimics: distinguish pericarditis from acute coronary syndrome (crushing, not positional), massive pulmonary embolism (sharp pleuritic but with DVT risk/desaturation), and costochondritis (reproducible on palpation) - the AI Doctor should recognise the pericarditic pattern but cannot exclude ACS/PE remotely

Concurrent myocarditis check: pericarditis with heart-failure signs (S3, raised JVP) or a large troponin rise (myopericarditis) raises acuity - here there are no such muscle signs, favouring uncomplicated pericarditis

NSAIDs ARE first-line here (contrast with myocarditis): high-dose NSAIDs plus colchicine and gastric protection are appropriate, but should be confirmed with an in-person ECG/troponin to exclude ACS and concurrent myocarditis
Tamponade/deterioration safety-netting: crushing pain, severe breathlessness at rest, syncope or leg swelling would signal ACS, effusion/tamponade or myocarditis needing emergency care

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-03)
